games.explanation: Shane Baz has a shiny strikeout number and a much uglier truth hiding underneath it, which is that right-handed bats have been teeing off on him all season. Washington happens to run a lineup full of righties who hit the ball extremely hard, with James Wood leading a group that's been barreling everything in sight against right-handed pitching. Baz's barrel rate against righties sits at a deeply uncomfortable 13 percent, and Littell, for all his contact-heavy quirks, gets to work against a Baltimore order that isn't exactly the '27 Yankees anymore. Getting plus money on the better projected team in a near coin flip is how the math is supposed to work, and at +125 the board is doing us a favor.

The market is pricing this like a coin flip between two similar teams, which ignores that one of these rosters is meaningfully better than the other. Tampa rolls out a top of the order where Aranda and Díaz have been doing real damage against right-handed pitching all season, and Janson Junk is the kind of contact-allowing righty who gives up a 47% hard-hit rate to anyone who isn't actively trying to ground out. Caminero adds the barrel risk that turns a four-run projection into a five-run inning. At -115 you're getting the stronger team, the better lineup, and the more vulnerable starter, which is more than the price suggests you're paying for.

Wacha at home in a wind-knocking-down park sounds like a problem until you remember he's the same guy who lets righties square him up all year, and St. Louis happens to run a righty-heavy order that's been doing exactly that to this profile. Herrera in particular is the kind of bat that turns a soft-contact starter into a long evening, and Burleson keeps the line moving against right-handers without giving at-bats away. Dustin May is the obvious counter, but the gap in team quality is wide enough (Elo has the Cards as comfortably the better side) that a pick'em price reads like a gift. At -110 on the stronger roster against the more hittable arm, the market is splitting a fight it shouldn't.
